content.problemStatement:
Abhishek is working in one of the best telecommunication companies of the world. He joined this company recently. To test his skills, his boss assigned him to help two of their customers: Aryan and Ali.

Initially both of their phone numbers are empty (fantasy world you see). During each day , one of the following things can take place:


Last digit of numbers of either Aryan or Ali can be erased.
A new digit can be appended at the end of the numbers of either Aryan or Ali.
The company also has a magical number MMM of length NNN, where each digit is between 000 and 999 (both inclusive). The positions of digits are numbered from 111 to NNN, from left to right. Aryan and Ali will be happy if both of their numbers appear as non-overlapping subsequences of MMM.

More formally, suppose that Aryan chooses some positions from MMM such that the digits at those positions form his current number (in the same order).Let the positions be a1,a2,a3,...,apa_{1},a_{2},a_{3},...,a_{p}a1​,a2​,a3​,...,ap​ . Now , Ali chooses some positions, not already chosen by Aryan such that the digits at those positions form his current number (in the same order).Let the positions be b1,b2,b3,...,bqb_{1},b_{2},b_{3},...,b_{q}b1​,b2​,b3​,...,bq​. Then the following conditions should hold true :


aia_{i}ai​ as well as bib_{i}bi​ are strictly increasing sequences.
For all pairs (ai,bj)(a_{i},b_{j})(ai​,bj​) 1≤i≤p1 \leq i \leq p1≤i≤p, 1≤j≤q1 \leq j \leq q1≤j≤q, ai≠bja_{i} \neq b_{j}ai​=bj​.
Values of aia_{i}ai​  are pairwise distinct. 1≤i≤p1 \leq i \leq p1≤i≤p.
Values of bib_{i}bi​  are pairwise distinct. 1≤i≤q1 \leq i \leq q1≤i≤q.
Ma1Ma2Ma3....MapM_{a_{1}}M_{a_{2}}M_{a_{3}}....M_{a_{p}}Ma1​​Ma2​​Ma3​​....Map​​ forms Aryan's number.
Mb1Mb2Mb3....MbqM_{b_{1}}M_{b_{2}}M_{b_{3}}....M_{b_{q}}Mb1​​Mb2​​Mb3​​....Mbq​​ forms Ali's number.
Over a period of DDD days, Abhishek has to follow one of the following instructions each day:


appendappendappend xxx yyy : You have to append digit yyy to the end of xxx's number.
eraseeraseerase xxx : Erase the last digit from xxx's number.
Abhishek has to help these customers over a period of DDD days. As he is a new employee, he asks for your help.
For each of the DDD days, you have to tell whether the phone numbers of Aryan and Ali appear as non-overlapping subsequences of MMM.

**NOTE** : Prefer using fast I/O methods, as the input is large.


Input Format

First line will contain two spaced integers NNN , DDD
The next line will contain the magical number MMM of length NNN
DDD lines follow. Each of these lines will contain either of the following:

appendappendappend xxx yyy : You have to append digit yyy to the end of xxx's number.
eraseeraseerase xxx : Erase the last digit from xxx's number.




Output Format

Print DDD lines. On the ithi^{th}ith line, print "YES" (without quotes) if both Aryan and Ali will be happy after the ithi^{th}ith day otherwise print "NO" (without quotes). You can print any character of the answer in any case, ie "yes", "YeS", "nO","No" etc will be considered valid response.
constraints: Length of Aryan's number ≤ 10^3 at any moment
Length of Ali's number ≤ 10^3 at any moment
Each digit of M and of the appended numbers is an integer between 0 and 999 inclusive
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers N and D – the length of the magical number M and the number of days. The second line contains N integers (each between 0 and 999) representing M. Then D lines follow, each describing an operation:
- "append Aryan x" – append digit x (0‑999) to Aryan's current number.
- "append Ali x" – append digit x to Ali's current number.
- "erase Aryan" – erase the last digit of Aryan's number (it is guaranteed to be non‑empty).
- "erase Ali" – erase the last digit of Ali's number.
All numbers may contain leading zeros.
content.outputFormat: Print D lines. After each operation print "YES" if both Aryan's and Ali's numbers can be found as non‑overlapping subsequences of M, otherwise print "NO".

sampleTestCases.input:
4 4
0013
append Aryan 1
append Ali 0
append Ali 3
append Aryan 9
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
At the end of first day, Aryan's number is 111 , while Ali's number is empty. Since 111 can be obtained as a subsequence of MMM, Aryan is happy. Ali is also happy(as his number is empty).


At the end of second day, numbers of Aryan and Ali are 111 and 000 respectively. Aryan can choose the subsequence formed by the digit at position 333 while Ali can choose the digit at position 111 . Since these are distinct positions , both will be happy.


At the end of third day, numbers of Aryan and Ali are 111 and 030303 respectively. Aryan can choose the same position as before, while Ali can choose positions 111 and 444. As these are distinct positions, both will be happy.


At the end of forth day, numbers of Aryan and Ali are 191919 and 030303 respectively. Since Aryan cannot choose any subsequence , he will be unhappy (Ali still being happy).

sampleTestCases.input:
7 10
9784831
append Aryan 9
append Ali 7
append Aryan 8
append Aryan 1
append Ali 8
append Ali 4
append Ali 4
append Ali 0
erase Ali
erase Ali
sampleTestCases.output:
YES
YES
YES
YES
YES
YES

YES

solution.approach:
Let A be Aryan's current string (length a) and B be Ali's current string (length b). For every pair (i,j) (0≤i≤a, 0≤j≤b) we store dp[i][j] – the smallest index in M (1‑based) after which we can match the first i characters of A and the first j characters of B as disjoint subsequences. dp[0][0]=0. The transition is:
    dp[i][j] = min( nextPos(dp[i‑1][j], A[i]), nextPos(dp[i][j‑1], B[j]) )
where nextPos(p, d) is the first position > p in M that contains digit d (or INF if none). The table is monotone, therefore when a new character is appended to A we only need to compute the new row dp[a][*] (O(b) work); when a character is appended to B we compute the new column dp[*][b] (O(a) work). Erasing simply reduces the effective length – the previously computed rows/columns stay valid. After each operation the answer is YES iff dp[a][b] ≤ N.
To obtain nextPos quickly we store for every digit (0‑999) the sorted list of its positions in M and answer a query with binary search (upper_bound). The total number of DP updates over the whole process is at most D·1000 ≤ 5·10^7, each requiring a binary search on a list of size ≤ N, which easily fits the limits.
The algorithm uses O(a·b) ≤ 10^6 memory for the DP table and O(N) memory for the position lists.
solution.timeComplexity: Each append costs O(otherLength·log N) (≤ 1000·log N). Erase is O(1). Over all D operations the total time is O(D·1000·log N) ≤ about 5·10^7·log(5·10^5), well within limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing positions of each digit (total N integers) + O(1000·1000) ≈ 1 MB for the DP table.
